questions.question: Explain why private interface methods were introduced in Java 9 and how they differ from default methods.
keyPoints: Private methods were introduced to enable code reuse within interfaces without exposing implementation details
Default methods are inherited and can be overridden by implementing classes, while private methods are not
Private methods solve the code duplication problem when multiple default methods share common logic
Private methods act as helper methods visible only within the interface itself

questions.question: Write a Java interface demonstrating the use of private methods to avoid code duplication in default methods.
keyPoints: Create an interface with two or more default methods
Extract common logic into a private method
Show the private method being called from default methods
Include appropriate return types and method signatures

questions.question: Can private interface methods be abstract? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: No, private interface methods cannot be abstract
This is because they must have a body/implementation to be callable
Abstract methods have no implementation and only declare a contract
Private methods are meant to be implementation details used internally

questions.question: What are the restrictions on private interface methods in Java 9+?
keyPoints: They must have an implementation (cannot be abstract)
They cannot be overridden by implementing classes
They are only accessible within the interface where they are declared
They cannot be used in the public API or called by external classes

questions.question: Explain the role of private interface methods in achieving encapsulation.
keyPoints: Private methods hide internal implementation details
They keep the public interface clean and focused on the contract
Implementing classes cannot see or depend on internal helper methods
Changes to private methods do not affect implementing classes - loose coupling

questions.question: Create an interface called 'DatabaseOperations' with multiple default methods for connect, query, and disconnect operations. Use private methods to handle common tasks like validation, logging, and resource cleanup. Write the complete Java code and explain how private methods improve code maintainability.
keyPoints: Define interface with private helper methods for validation, logging, and cleanup
Create default methods that use these private helpers internally
Show code without duplication - common logic extracted to private methods
Explain maintainability benefits: single point of change, hidden implementation details
Demonstrate that implementing classes only see public default methods

questions.question: Compare and contrast public, default, static, and private methods in Java interfaces. Include syntax, inheritance behavior, overriding rules, and use cases for each.
keyPoints: Public methods: abstract by default, define contract, inherited and overridable
Default methods: have implementation, inherited and optionally overridable, introduced in Java 8
Static methods: belong to interface, called via InterfaceName.method(), not inherited, introduced in Java 8
Private methods: have implementation, not inherited, not overridable, helper methods, introduced in Java 9
Use cases: public for contract, default for backward compatibility, static for utility functions, private for internal code reuse

questions.question: Design an interface 'PaymentProcessor' that demonstrates the evolution of interface capabilities from Java 7 to Java 9. Show how each version added new capabilities and explain a real-world scenario where private methods in Java 9 solve a practical problem.
keyPoints: Java 7: Only abstract methods - show simple interface
Java 8: Default and static methods added - extend the interface
Java 9: Private methods - show common code extraction
Real-world scenario: Multiple payment types (CreditCard, PayPal, UPI) with validation logic
Show how private methods avoid duplicating validation in each default method
Explain benefits: easier maintenance, no exposure of internal validation rules
